section.title: Weekly Routine
section.p: This course has a flipped design. That means that I rely on you to watch some lessons and do some reading before coming to class, not after. Then we can spend class time more productively. You will learn more, remember it longer, and enjoy our time in the classroom more this way. However it only works well if you make plans for watching the videos and doing the reading before class.

Sometime before a given class that covers certain sections, you should:
watch the video lessons we provide for those sections
take notes as you watch them, using the video lecture note outlines we provide
read those textbook sections and complete the interactive Checkpoint items as you go
For each class, this will take about 3 hours of your time. So it takes around 6 hours per week. The reward will be more interesting things to do during class, and ultimately less homework for you.

Our class time takes 5 hours of your time each week. Beyond this, you have up to 5 hours of homework each week. In total that is up to 16 hours of your time each week, which is appropriate for this 4-credit course.

You have two forms of homework to practice the material:
Online homework in Runestone Academy that is tied to the textbook. This includes the Checkpoint exercises, the Reading Questions, and some of the exercises at the end of each section.
In class we do worksheets and group activities. If you complete them in class, great. However you may need more time outside of class to complete some of these. Or you might miss class. These worksheets and activities are also part of your course grade. They help you learn and practice math, but also help us work together on your math writing skills.
These assignments (Runestone and the previous week's in-class activities) are due Sundays at 10:00 . When needed, you can scan and post your work in D2L. If you miss a due date, you still have two weeks to catch up without any effect on your grade. But this two-week grace period shortens right before a test. For example our chapter 1 exam is on a Monday, and all chapter 1 assignments have a hard close date the day before that exam.
